explicit_text: I recall a young woman who had just finished at Duke University and was working on the paper downtown during my first year there. She came out to interview me but was unusually nervous, and the recording machine failed to work. Despite that rocky start, she later served briefly as my press secretary, and we got along well.

She came out to interview me but was unusually nervous, and the recording machine failed to work. Despite that rocky start, she later served briefly as my press secretary, and we got along well.

explicit_text: I've done many interviews and writings about my career, including one for the LBJ Library. I was reading the transcripts of that interview, which helped me organize this current one to cover some old ground from different angles. The LBJ Library interview is part of my extensive reflections on my life and work.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Laurinburg, North Carolina, on Church Street, and lived there until I went away to college and through the war. Laurinburg was a dusty little farm trading town with mostly dirt roads and a sleepy atmosphere, typical of the 1930s. I remember every house and person, having spent much time selling magazines and delivering telegraphs there. The town was dominated financially by the McNair family but was filled with great, tough people mostly of Scottish heritage.

explicit_text: As a young boy in Laurinburg, I spent a lot of time selling the Saturday Evening Post along with the Ladies' Home Journal. This was part of my early work experiences in the town during the 1920s and 1930s. Selling these magazines helped me become familiar with the community and its people.

This was part of my early work experiences in the town during the 1920s and 1930s. Selling these magazines helped me become familiar with the community and its people.

explicit_text: Alongside the Saturday Evening Post, I sold the Ladies' Home Journal in Laurinburg during my youth. This was part of my routine as I got to know the town and its residents well. These magazine sales were a significant part of my childhood memories in the 1920s and 1930s.

explicit_text: In Laurinburg, I also delivered telegraphs, initially Postal telegraphs and later Western Union telegraphs. This was part of my early work and gave me insight into the communication systems of the time. Delivering Western Union messages connected me to many people in the community during the 1930s.

explicit_text: The Great Depression deeply affected Laurinburg and my family. My father's hardware business went broke around 1929, one of the early victims of the economic downturn. Despite the hardships, many in Laurinburg, including the McNair family who acquired much farmland during the Hoover depression, managed to get through it fairly well.
implicit_text: The economic downturn of the late 1920s and 1930s deeply affected my hometown and my family. My father's hardware business went broke around 1929, one of the early victims of the economic collapse. Despite the hardships, many in the town, including the prominent local family who acquired much farmland during the Hoover administration's depression, managed to get through it fairly well.

explicit_text: The McNair family was the dominant financial force in Laurinburg during my youth, owning the bank, farmland, a flour mill, cottonseed oil plant, and textile mills. They acquired much of their land when farms went broke during the Hoover depression. Though influential, they were modest and not overbearing, and their legacy continues through descendants with names like Evans, Jones, and McCoy.

explicit_text: During the 1930s, my father worked for the Works Progress Administration when jobs were scarce. The WPA also undertook projects in Laurinburg, like digging out swamps to improve drainage, which changed the landscape I remembered from childhood. These efforts were part of the New Deal programs aimed at providing employment and improving infrastructure.

This agency also undertook projects in my hometown, like digging out swamps to improve drainage, which changed the landscape I remembered from childhood. These efforts were part of the federal programs aimed at providing employment and improving infrastructure.

explicit_text: Newton was a section of Laurinburg where many black residents lived in very poor conditions, often in shacks without indoor toilets. It was locally called 'Nigger Town' but politely referred to as Newtown. I sold vegetables there and developed a deep concern for people left out of the system, witnessing both their resilience and hardship during the 1930s.

It was locally called by a racial slur but politely referred to as Newtown. I sold vegetables there and developed a deep concern for people left out of the system, witnessing both their resilience and hardship during the 1930s.

explicit_text: I have seen conditions in Chicago that reminded me of the poverty and human waste I observed in Newton, Laurinburg. Though I wasn't focused on migration patterns, I noted that many black people moved to northern cities like Chicago during the Great Migration. The harsh living conditions in some Chicago neighborhoods paralleled those in the South.

Though I wasn't focused on migration patterns, I noted that many black people moved to northern cities like this one during the Great Migration. The harsh living conditions in some neighborhoods there paralleled those in the South.

explicit_text: Harlem, like Chicago, had areas with severe poverty and poor sanitation that reminded me of the conditions in Newton, North Carolina. These urban neighborhoods faced challenges such as human waste in the streets and lack of proper facilities. My observations of Harlem contributed to my understanding of the plight of marginalized communities in both the South and the North.

explicit_text: I remember many white families from Laurinburg moving to Detroit in search of better-paying jobs in the automobile industry during the 1920s and 1930s. One family of Coopers, with Revolutionary War ties, had several brothers who went up there and did very well. This migration was part of the broader movement of people seeking industrial employment in northern cities.

explicit_text: My family has deep roots going back to the Revolutionary War, including the Coopers who moved to Detroit. My grandfather's father was killed during the American Civil War, but the Revolutionary War heritage was a point of pride in our family history. These historical ties shaped my understanding of my family's place in American history.

explicit_text: My grandfather, James D. Sanford, came to Laurinburg as a cabinetmaker working for the Seaboard Air Line Railroad. This was after his father was killed in the Civil War when he was about ten or eleven years old. The railroad provided him with employment and a means to settle in Laurinburg.

explicit_text: James D. Sanford was my grandfather who came to Laurinburg as a cabinetmaker with the Seaboard Air Line Railroad. His father died in the Civil War when James was very young. He ran a hardware store that eventually failed around 1929, and he lived until about eighty-five, passing away while I was in college.

explicit_text: My mother came from Virginia, specifically Salem, and came from a fairly affluent family. She was a schoolteacher before moving to Laurinburg and returned to teaching when I was in fifth grade during the early 1930s. Her family background and teaching career influenced my upbringing and social standing.

explicit_text: My father taught many young people in Sunday school who were Methodists, which was a significant part of our community life in Laurinburg. Methodism provided a social and religious framework that connected families and individuals. It was part of the social acceptability and community involvement in our town.
